data.title: Vita Coco expects $790M-$805M net sales and $154M-$161M adjusted EBITDA as it adds Copra
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: The Vita Coco Company (COCO) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “I’m pleased to announce the acquisition of Copra Inc.” (Co-Founder, Executive Chairman & President Michael Kirban) and “we estimate that this super premium cold segment represents approximately 13% of U.S. coconut water sales,” with Copra positioned as “the leading supplier of private label products for this segment.”
  • “Our net sales in the quarter were up 28%, driven by the strong growth of Vita Coco Coconut Water of 21% and growth in private label of 83%.” (CEO & Director Martin Roper) He also said, “we lost several weeks of production…representing approximately 1% of our full network annual production” due to the General Santos, Philippines earthquake.
  • “For the second quarter, net sales increased $47 million or 28% year-over-year to $216 million.” (CFO & Interim Chief Accounting Officer Corey Baker) He added, “gross margins finished at 49% for the quarter,” and “tariff refunds improved gross margin within the quarter by approximately 700 basis points.”

Outlook

  • “We now expect net sales between $790 million and $805 million.” (CFO Baker)
  • “We expect gross margin for the full year of approximately 40%, delivering adjusted EBITDA of $154 million to $161 million.” (CFO Baker)
  • “We now expect full year private label net sales growth of 90% to 100% in the U.S.” (CFO Baker) and “consolidated growth of Vita Coco Coconut Water net sales of high teens to 20%.”
  • Versus Q1, management raised full-year guidance and added Copra; in Q1, the company said tariff refunds were not assumed, while in Q2 the company incorporated them into guidance.

Financial Results

  • “Net income attributable to shareholders for the quarter was $49 million or $0.82 per diluted share.” (CFO Baker)
  • “Q2 2026 adjusted EBITDA was $67 million or 31% of net sales.” (CFO Baker)
  • “As of June 30, 2026…total cash on hand of $279 million and no debt under our revolving credit facility.” (CFO Baker) He also said, “in July, our Board approved a $40 million increase in our stock buyback authorization…$61 million remaining under the total $105 million authorized.”
  • On Copra deal terms, “the first payment of $175 million…consisting of approximately 80% cash and 20% stock,” plus “additional consideration…in 2029…a floor of $45 million and a cap of $100 million.” (CFO Baker) He added, “Copra projects their full year calendar net sales for 2026 to be above $100 million.”

Q&A

  • Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division: Asked why guidance implies 2H deceleration and margin deleverage; CFO Baker: “we do run into the inventory builds at distributor, Walmart load-in,” and “inflation is starting to hit Q3, Q4,” while Copra integration drove “a bit of conservativeness on the SG&A.”
  • Peter Galbo, BofA Securities, Research Division: Asked whether Copra capacity can support Vita Coco; Executive Chairman Kirban: “it’s a different actual liquid than what is in Vita Coco,” so “the 2 capacity builds…they’re separate.” Galbo also asked about shipments vs. scans; CFO Baker: “it’s just a shipment timing item, primarily with the MBM.”
  • Christopher Carey, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: Asked about back-half category assumptions; CFO Baker: “we’re basing the guidance on the 20s, low 20s.” On Copra brand vs private label and margins, Executive Chairman Kirban said the goal is “eventually be the largest player in that segment…through mostly brand,” while CFO Baker said, “it’s operating with a large percentage of private label…lower gross margin.” Carey asked Copra profitability; Executive Chairman Kirban: “No, it’s a profitable business, and it’s included.”
  • Jon Andersen, William Blair & Company L.L.C., Research Division: Asked private label strength and run-rate; CEO Roper cited “regain regions and the new tetra customer,” and Europe where “Germany…is exploding.” CFO Baker added launch benefits from “a distribution pipeline fill.” Andersen asked EBITDA bridge; CFO Baker: “the tariffs were not in previously, they’re now in.”
  • Eric Serotta, Morgan Stanley, Research Division: Asked why buy Copra now; Executive Chairman Kirban cited super premium growth and said the deal brings “a…manufacturing opportunity and a supply chain opportunity.” Serotta asked about freight; CEO Roper: “we still view that there is excess capacity versus demand,” and increases would flow “in Q3 and…Q4.”
  • Eric Des Lauriers, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC, Research Division: Asked super premium private label dynamics; Executive Chairman Kirban: “they’re providing both” shelf-stable and refrigerated offerings. CEO Roper said Nam Hom private label sells “at 2x what Vita Coco does on a per liter basis,” and pricing will likely be “sort of on a cost-plus basis.” On vertical integration, CEO Roper said the current model remains “asset-light…for at least the next couple of years.”
  • James Salera, Stephens Inc., Research Division: Asked if guidance is capped by capacity; CEO Roper: “we are currently running closer to 95% capacity,” and capacity loss from the earthquake was “about 1%.” On 2027 flex, CEO Roper: “there always is, and we’re talking.”
  • Kaumil Gajrawala, Jefferies: Asked whether Nam Hom sourcing can scale; Executive Chairman Kirban: “there is capacity to be had today, significant capacity.” He also said the product “ships chilled and stays chilled all the way through the supply chain.”
  • Robert Ottenstein, Evercore ISI Institutional Equities, Research Division: Asked super premium demographics and why private label is prominent; CEO Roper said consumers are “pretty similar…maybe a little higher income and a little older,” and private label strength is “partially related to Costco.” On Costco private label supplier, CEO Roper: “we would never comment publicly on who provides private label.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to pressing on 2H deceleration, margins, and integration risk, including “your guidance…implies a deceleration” (Bonnie Herzog, Goldman Sachs Group, Inc., Research Division) and repeated probing on Copra economics.
  • Management tone was positive and confident in demand and strategy, including “our confidence in the category and Vita Coco brand trends remains very high.” (CEO Roper) Management also acknowledged uncertainty on inflation: “it is unclear how long these [freight] surcharges will remain effective.” (CEO Roper)
  • Versus Q1, management’s confidence remained high, but Q2 added more operational risk detail (earthquake disruption) and more explicit discussion of cost inflation timing into Q3/Q4.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The largest strategic change was the Q2 Copra acquisition; Q1 emphasized an “asset-light model,” while Q2 added ownership of a Thailand facility and committed Copra CapEx (“approximately $11 million”).
  • Guidance shifted materially upward (net sales and adjusted EBITDA ranges increased) and incorporated tariff refunds that Q1 explicitly excluded from guidance.
  • Q1 freight coverage was described as “approximately 25%,” while Q2 said new contracts “provide us with approximately 50% coverage” for the balance of the year.
  • Analyst questioning shifted from Q1 phasing and promotions to Q2 integration execution, super premium segment sizing, cold-chain logistics, and capacity ceilings.

Risks and Concerns

  • Cost inflation and margin pressure: “Most of these increases will start impacting our gross margin mid-third quarter.” (CEO Roper)
  • Supply disruption: “We lost several weeks of production…representing approximately 1% of our full network annual production.” (CEO Roper)
  • Capacity tightness: “We are currently running closer to 95% capacity.” (CEO Roper)
  • Integration uncertainty: “It just kind of closed yesterday…we are cautious in trying to understand the integration costs.” (CFO Baker)

Final Takeaway

Management described accelerating coconut water demand and share gains across major markets, raised full-year 2026 guidance to $790 million-$805 million in net sales and $154 million-$161 million in adjusted EBITDA, and framed the Copra acquisition as a manufacturing-led entry into the refrigerated super premium Nam Hom segment, while flagging second-half gross-margin pressure from packaging, logistics, and ocean-freight inflation alongside near-term capacity constraints and integration execution risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Visteon targets $6B 2026 bookings while outlining $200M accelerated share repurchase
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Visteon (VC) Q2 2026

Management view

  • “Customer vehicle production declined approximately 5% during the quarter, yet our sales remained essentially flat year-over-year, resulting in approximately 4 percentage points of market outperformance,” said “Adjusted EBITDA was $116 million, representing a 12.1% margin,” and added, “this morning, we announced a $200 million accelerated share repurchase program,” (President, CEO & Director Sachin Lawande).

  • “We launched 24 new products across 11 automakers and secured $2 billion of new business awards, bringing first half bookings to $3 billion and keeping us on track for our full year $6 billion target,” and said Visteon “expanded our SmartCore high-performance compute business with another premium brand under the Geely Group,” (President, CEO & Director Lawande).

  • “For the quarter, sales were $960 million, down 1% from the prior year,” and “Adjusted free cash flow was $20 million,” while noting “we completed the acquisition of an engineering service company for $20 million,” (Senior VP & CFO Jerome Rouquet).

Outlook

  • “We expect Visteon sales to grow in the second half compared to prior year,” even as “customer vehicle production [is] forecasted to be down by about 5%,” with “sales…expected to grow in all regions, except in Americas,” (President, CEO & Director Lawande).

  • Europe: “we expect another period of strong execution with mid-teens sales growth,” including “start production of our SmartCore cockpit domain controller with a premium German OEM,” and China: “we expect to return to low single-digit sales growth as our first SmartCore HPC programs launch with Geely and Chery,” (President, CEO & Director Lawande).

  • “We are reaffirming our full year guidance across all key financial metrics,” including sales “between $3.625 billion and $3.825 billion and are trending towards the high end of the range at $3.8 billion,” adjusted EBITDA “between $455 million and $495 million…approximately $475 million,” and adjusted free cash flow “between $170 million to $210 million…trending towards the low end…$170 million,” (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet).

Financial results

  • “Adjusted EBITDA was $116 million or 12.1% for the quarter, our best EBITDA margin since Q3 of 2025,” and Rouquet attributed sequential margin improvement to “the recoveries we secured in the quarter, combined with strong cost discipline.” (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet).

  • Rouquet said Q2 free cash flow stayed positive despite working-capital actions: “Adjusted free cash flow was $20 million, positive for the quarter despite an increase in inventory as we continue to build resilience in our supply chain,” and he added the quarter included “the timing of cash settlements of previously accrued tax expenses.” (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet).

  • On balance sheet and capital structure, Rouquet said, “We ended the quarter with $650 million of cash and net cash of $351 million,” and “completed the refinancing of our $300 million term loan facility and $400 million revolving credit facility, extending the maturity to 2031.” (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet).

Q&A

  • Thomas Ito, RBC Capital Markets: asked about Chinese OEM cockpit in-sourcing risk; President, CEO & Director Lawande said, “they're actively collaborating with strategic suppliers,” and added, “the sales plan that we presented at Investor Day…we didn't include any unsubstantiated sales just based on Ford.”

  • Thomas Ito, RBC Capital Markets: asked why not more aggressive buybacks; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet said, “our net cash target was $150 million,” and “the rationale for the ASR…$200 million that will allow us to retire shares pretty quickly.”

  • Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan: asked about the Micron agreement; President, CEO & Director Lawande said it provides “supply assurance, pricing predictability and better planning,” but added, “we anticipate 2027 to be quite challenging in terms of getting sufficient supply to meet our demand.”

  • Rajat Gupta, JPMorgan: asked early SmartCore margin read; President, CEO & Director Lawande said, “the launch margins are going to be a little lower than the steady state,” and “we expect them to be very similar to our average margins.”

  • Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe Research: pressed on OEM in-sourcing “sort of like happening”; President, CEO & Director Lawande responded, “I wouldn't say that that's the right characterization,” and added Visteon is “not seeing anything different than what we've seen in the past.”

  • Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe Research: asked about inflation beyond memory; Senior VP & CFO Rouquet said memory impact was “approximately 2.5% of our sales,” and added, “we've seen some other inflation costs…starting at the beginning of the second quarter.”

  • Joseph Spak, UBS: challenged why recovery can’t move toward full pass-through; President, CEO & Director Lawande said, “we fully anticipate to go out to our customers to recover 100% of the cost increases next year,” while distinguishing “non-memory-related semiconductors.”

  • Itay Michaeli, TD Cowen: asked about the new Japanese OEM win; President, CEO & Director Lawande said, “we have never been a supplier to this OEM,” and added “there are…three vehicles in the initial award with more to follow.”

  • Dan Levy, Barclays: asked China underperformance and the second-half growth “flip”; President, CEO & Director Lawande said China demand is shifting toward the “premium tech segment,” and “this dynamic changes as we go into the second half with the launches…HPC.”

  • Yan Dong, Deutsche Bank: asked if the new HPC win is incremental; President, CEO & Director Lawande said, “the HPC win that we announced is incremental to what we had assumed for HPC sales.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly negative, repeatedly pressing on OEM in-sourcing, China visibility, and component inflation, including “it feels like…it's sort of like happening” on in-sourcing (Emmanuel Rosner, Wolfe Research) and detailed recovery math challenges (Joseph Spak, UBS).

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive in Q&A on in-sourcing; Lawande said, “I wouldn't say that that's the right characterization,” while Rouquet used confidence framing on guidance: “We are reaffirming our full year guidance across all key financial metrics.”

  • Versus Q1, the discussion shifted from recovery timing and production forecasts to capital return execution (ASR) and a more explicit 2027 memory supply challenge; in both quarters management reiterated confidence in full-year guidance.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q2 added a new capital return action: “a $200 million accelerated share repurchase agreement,” compared with Q1’s smaller quarterly return (“$40 million through share repurchases and dividends”) and a then-open question about authorization capacity. (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet; Q1: President, CEO & Director Lawande).

  • Q2 reiterated the full-year framework but updated stance on trajectory: sales were described as “trending towards the high end…$3.8 billion,” while free cash flow was framed as “trending towards the low end…$170 million,” versus Q1’s emphasis that free cash flow was “currently trending towards the lower end” due to elevated inventories. (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet).

  • China and HPC commentary in Q2 leaned more on second-half launches and domestic OEM mix (“close to 60% indexed with domestic OE in China”), whereas Q1 emphasized the premium-tech AI thesis and described a “more meaningful step-up in 2027.” (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet; Q1: President, CEO & Director Lawande).

Risks and concerns

  • Lawande warned, “we anticipate 2027 to be quite challenging in terms of getting sufficient supply to meet our demand,” and said mitigation includes “multiple alternate suppliers,” “redesigning some of the products,” and the Micron agreement for “better assurance on supply.” (President, CEO & Director Lawande).

  • Rouquet said cost pressure is broadening: “cost pressures initially seen in memory are now extending to other purchase components,” and outlined mitigation as “recoveries and the ramp-up of our cost initiatives across product costing, vertical integration and engineering productivity.” (Senior VP & CFO Rouquet).

  • On China, Lawande cited “continued weakness in the value segment…following the changes in government policies and incentives,” while positioning exposure toward “premium domestic manufacturers.” (President, CEO & Director Lawande).

Final takeaway

Management characterized Q2 as a quarter of market outperformance despite lower customer production, a step-up in profitability versus Q1 driven by customer recoveries and cost actions, and an acceleration of capital returns through a $200 million ASR. Leadership reaffirmed full-year sales, EBITDA, and free cash flow ranges while calling out tightening component cost pressures and a potentially more difficult 2027 memory supply environment, alongside mitigation actions spanning supplier agreements, product redesigns, and additional sourcing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Volkswagen posts €82.44B Q2 revenue, cuts full-year sales growth outlook
data.description:
• Volkswagen Group (VWAGY) reported second-quarter revenue of €82.44 billion, representing a 2.0% increase year-over-year, while trimming its full-year sales outlook to reflect softer overall delivery volumes in the first half of 2026.
  • Order bank in Europe up by around 12% compared with year-end 2025.
  • 4.0 million vehicle sales in H1 2026, 8.4% below H1 2025 (4.4 million vehicles).
  • Outlook: The Volkswagen Group expects sales revenue in 2026 to develop within a range of –3 to 0 percent compared with the previous year (previously: range of 0 to +3 percent). The Group’s operating return on sales is still expected to be between 4.0 and 5.5 percent. In the Automotive Division, the company continues to expect an investment ratio of between 11 and 12 percent in 2026. Net cash flow for the year 2026 is expected to range between EUR 3 billion and EUR 6 billion. Net liquidity in the Automotive Division is expected to range between EUR 32 billion and EUR 34 billion in 2026. The Volkswagen Group continues to pursue its objective of maintaining a solid financing and liquidity policy.

data.title: Spiking oil prices weigh on gold as investors brace for next week's Fed meeting
data.description:
Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) slipped further on Friday in Asian trading following a 2% drop in the prior session, as Brent crude (CO1:COM) rising above $100 a barrel renewed inflation worries and bolstered expectations for higher interest rates ahead of next week's Fed meeting.

Spot gold eased 0.6% to $4,025.38 per ounce at press time, with prices down more than $130 from a two-week high hit on Wednesday. Bullion was on track for a small weekly gain of 0.4%.

“In the short term, we expect more volatility… gold has been trading between $3,980 all the way to about $4,170 and has been stuck in this way for weeks. Every time prices hit close to $4,000 or slightly below, we see that there will be big buyers coming to buy it back up,” GoldSilver Central Managing Director Brian Lan told Reuters.

U.S. President Donald Trump promised on Thursday “major military punishment” for Iran and its Houthi allies after the Yemeni fighters struck two Saudi oil tankers in the Red Sea.

Brent crude surged 7% on Thursday, topping $100 a barrel for the first time since May—its sharpest spike since the war began—reigniting fears of persistent inflation and sustained high-interest rates.

While gold is traditionally considered a hedge against inflation, it loses its appeal as a non-yielding asset in a high-interest-rate environment.

Investors are also turned toward next week's U.S. Federal Reserve meeting, with the central bank widely expected to hold interest rates steady. The European Central Bank kept interest rates unchanged as expected on Thursday but held the door open to another increase in September.

Among other precious metals, spot silver (XAGUSD:CUR) was down 0.5% at $57.34 per ounce but was headed for a weekly gain of 2.5%. Platinum (XPTUSD:CUR) fell 1.3% to $1,587.80, and palladium (XPDUSD:CUR) slipped 2.1% to $1,237.50, both on track for a weekly drop.

data.title: Codexis falls 20% after pricing $25M stock offering at $1.50/share
data.description:
• Codexis (CDXS) priced a public offering of 16.67M common shares at $1.50/share, at the bottom of its marketed range.
  • Gross proceeds are expected to be approximately $25M, with net proceeds of about $23.1M after underwriting discounts and commissions.
  • The offering is expected to close on July 27, 2026.
  • The proceeds will be used for general corporate purposes, including working capital and advancing the company's business strategy.
  • The stock price slumped about 20% on Thursday after market hours.

data.title: JetBlue bonds sink as airline schedules investor call on liquidity: report
data.description:
• JetBlue Airways (JBLU) reportedly bonds dropped ahead of next week’s Q2 earnings report, driven by renewed investor jitters over economic headwinds.
  • The $2 billion in bonds due 2031 fell more than 2 cents to about 84 cents on the dollar, the lowest price since their issuance in August 2024.
  • The move came after the budget airline invited fixed income investors to a meeting focused on the company’s liquidity and balance sheet in August, according to Bloomberg. A JetBlue spokesman said it regularly meets with investors to discuss strategy and performance.

data.title: Asian stocks fall across the board as tech sell-off and $92 oil weigh on sentiment
data.description:
Asian stock markets traded in red on Friday tracking a sharp selloff on Wall Street as investors voiced growing skepticism over whether heavy investments in artificial intelligence would translate into near-term profitability.

Concurrently, U.S. equity index futures were little changed after a broad market selloff driven by soaring oil prices and disappointing earnings from major technology companies. Nasdaq 100 futures fell 0.19%, while S&P 500 was flat and Dow Jones futures rose 0.08%.

In commodities, crude oil traded above $92 per barrel, while spot gold prices fell toward $4,030 per ounce.

The benchmark KOSPI fell 4.95% to around 6,710, snapping a three-day rally as a selloff in technology stocks. The South Korean won 

Japan's (NKY:IND) fell 2.75% to below 64,900, while the Topix Index dropped over 1% to below 4,000 on Friday, reversing the previous session's gains. The Japanese yen remained under pressure, hovering near a four-decade low around 163.8 per US dollar.

Economic Data: Japan’s annual inflation rate accelerated to 1.7% in June from 1.5% in May, with core CPI rising to 1.6% year-on-year. Meanwhile, the  S&P Global Composite PMI rose to 53.1 in July from 52.8 in June, hitting its highest level since February.

China's (SHCOMP) fell 1.28% to 3,853, while the Shenzhen Component lost 0.6% to 14,041, reversing the previous session’s gains. The offshore yuan 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 1.39% to 24,877, giving back part of the previous session's gains.

India (SENSEX) fell 1.09% to around 75,688 in early trading on Friday, extending its losing streak to a fifth consecutive session, while the rupee weakened to around 96.58 per dollar. 

Economic Data: The HSBC Flash India Manufacturing PMI dipped to 53.9 in July from 54.2 in June, pointing to a slight moderation in factory growth.

Australia (AS51) shares fell 0.71% to 8,813, reversing gains from the previous three sessions. The Australian dollar traded below $0.698 and remained on track for its first weekly decline in a month.

Economic Data: Australia’s private sector activity reached its highest level since January, propelled by a services rebound and the first surge in new factory orders in five months.

Currencies: (JPY:USD), (CNY:USD), (AUD:USD), (INR:USD), (HKD:USD), (NZD:USD).

data.title: SL Green projects $1.20-a-share FFO guidance increase, supported by $0.80 from One Vanderbilt accounting change
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SL Green (SLG) Q2 2026

Management View

  • “Our economic occupancy… certainly did this quarter, up a remarkable 300 basis points as concessions continue to burn off and overall vacancy dwindles,” Marc Holliday (Chairman & CEO) said, adding, “We now know that we'll exceed our leasing goals again this year.”

  • Holliday highlighted SUMMIT momentum despite softer city tourism: “We enjoyed the highest attendance amongst all of our competitors… and introduced a number of new ticketed experiences that we expect will continue to drive revenue,” and added, “We are on track to open Paris next summer in 2027 and in Tokyo in 2030.”

  • “We are excited to be able to translate these successes into a significant upward FFO guidance revision of $1.20 a share, more than 26%, the vast majority of which is recurring,” Matthew Diliberto (Chief Financial Officer) said.

  • Diliberto detailed drivers: “revenues benefit from strong leasing… along with a conscious effort to accelerate GAAP revenue recognition… coupled with phenomenal expense containment,” plus One Vanderbilt: “The total of these 2 components contributes an additional $0.80 a share of FFO in '26, $0.35 of which we recorded in the second quarter.”

Outlook

  • Management framed the update as a major shift in 2026 earnings expectations: Holliday called it “our big guidance revision for this quarter,” and said, “We've executed a deliberate strategy to invest what was needed to move our occupancy back towards 95%, and we're now reaching a positive inflection point.”

  • Diliberto linked the 2026 revision to both real estate operations and fee income: the company expects “$0.20 a share of incremental FFO in 2026 from the real estate portfolio” and “an additional $0.20 a share of FFO” from “fee and other income,” with the remainder tied to One Vanderbilt’s incremental FFO mechanics.

  • On cash flow coverage timing, Diliberto reiterated: “'27 into '28, with the breakeven point in '28 is still the path that we are on at this point,” later clarifying “breakeven… as against coverage of your dividend.”

Financial Results

  • The company did not provide EPS or revenue figures in the transcript; management focused on FFO guidance revision mechanics and operating indicators.

  • Diliberto attributed part of 2026 uplift to execution already visible in Q2: “to drive $0.20 a share of incremental FFO in 2026 from the real estate portfolio… $0.10 of which we recognized in the second quarter.”

  • One Vanderbilt accounting treatment was positioned as a recurring earnings contributor: “This amortization component alone is approximately $21 million a year through the early part of 2031,” Diliberto said, adding, “Going forward, every dollar of cash distribution out of One Vanderbilt… is incremental FFO to us.”

  • On capital structure posture amid rates, Diliberto said, “We remain more like 90-10” fixed-to-floating and “we are maintaining a very cautious stance when it comes to rates.”

Q&A

  • Nicholas Yulico, Scotiabank: asked what drove strong mark-to-market leasing; Steven Durels (Executive VP and Director of Leasing & Real Property) said, “it's a broad-based improvement… Anything on Park Avenue, we've raised rents dramatically… 1185 6th, rents are up dramatically.”

  • Yulico, Scotiabank: asked about unlocking One Vanderbilt mark-to-market; Durels said, “there are opportunities… recapturing some of those spaces… and the rents will be up.”

  • Alexander Goldfarb, Piper Sandler: asked why recovery is so fast; Holliday cited “almost no addition to space,” companies planning long-term, and conversions: “you have an inventory that's actually dropping.”

  • Goldfarb, Piper Sandler: asked if office-to-resi pipeline could reverse; Holliday said economics are “closing the gap,” but “still tilts in favor of conversion.”

  • Steve Sakwa, Evercore ISI: asked on refinancing/asset sales; Diliberto said SLG is “completed or in contract on 4 of the 11 deals,” expects “2 additional deals soon,” and said “the next one up in the queue is 245 Park.”

  • Sakwa, Evercore ISI: asked about 1515 Broadway after casino loss; Holliday said Paramount/Skydance developments “puts 1515 kind of squarely back in the mix,” and suggested more clarity “is next year.”

  • Thomas Catherwood, BTIG: asked embedded mark-to-market on COVID-era leases; Holliday gave “a broad range between 10% and 20%,” while Durels said many were short-term and “we don't have any big chunky expirations.”

  • Catherwood, BTIG: asked how the debt fund adapts as banks return; Harrison Sitomer (President & CIO) said deployments were “approximately $600 million,” and strategy is “financial engineering… build capital stacks to get to our yields.”

  • John Kim, BMO: asked what drove $0.20 operational uplift; Diliberto cited “renewals and early renewals,” faster turnover to “turn that earnings spigot on,” and expenses “ahead on expenses.”

  • Kim, BMO: asked how higher benchmarks affect refinancing; Sitomer cited CMBS market data and said SLG is “very confident in the execution” for 245 Park, while Diliberto emphasized hedging “well ahead of time.”

  • Blaine Heck, Wells Fargo: asked pipeline update; Durels said “roughly 50% new, 50% renewal,” with “400,000 square feet… in active negotiation.”

  • Heck, Wells Fargo: asked why JV with Mori at 346 Madison now; Holliday said, “I love fully capitalized deals,” and SLG “reserved enough… to probably syndicate equity further down the line.”

  • Peter Abramowitz, Deutsche Bank: asked if FAD inflection timing changes; Diliberto said trajectory is “slightly ahead,” but still “'27 into '28.”

  • Abramowitz, Deutsche Bank: asked FIFA impact on SUMMIT; Holliday said there was “definitely a bump,” noted “daily ticket sales exceeding 400 and some-odd thousand a day,” and expects to “finish up right on our numbers.”

  • Anthony Paolone, JPMorgan: asked cap rates for upcoming dispositions; Holliday cited completed deals: “10 East 53rd Street… 5.7% cap rate” and “7 Dey… 5.0%.”

  • Paolone, JPMorgan: asked 750 Third and 346 Madison issues; Holliday said “no interruption” at 750 and on 346 access litigation: “we anticipate no adverse outcome and no adverse impact on timeline.”

  • Seth Bergey, Citi: asked buybacks vs debt paydown; Holliday said SLG used buybacks when “the price was not nearly reflective of the underlying value,” and may do more depending on “the landscape.”

  • Bergey, Citi: asked about an alternate/core FFO metric; Diliberto said, “No. I don't believe in violating what NAREIT says is FFO.”

  • Vikram Malhotra, Mizuho: asked CapEx cadence and breakeven definition; Diliberto said CapEx is “a little back ended” and breakeven refers to “coverage of your dividend.”

  • Malhotra, Mizuho: asked cap rate ranges; Holliday said SLG assets are “decidedly between 5 and 6,” with “certain assets… sub-5.”

  • Malhotra, Mizuho: asked 2027 debt/swaps plan; Diliberto replied, “I plan to do that in December,” then “No.”

  • Ronald Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: asked commenced occupancy and 2027 NOI; Diliberto said SLG still expects “in excess of 10% same-store NOI, cash NOI growth in '27.”

  • Kamdem, Morgan Stanley: asked on certain alternative strategy assets; Holliday said they “need to be recapitalized,” contribute “little in the way of earnings,” and “we have no recourse to speak of.”

  • Brendan Lynch, Barclays: asked how low free rent can go; Durels said renewals are “3 to 4 months” on a typical 5-year deal, and for new 10-year deals “get down to kind of the 10-month free rent.”

  • Caitlin Burrows, Goldman Sachs: asked why One Vanderbilt uplift discussed now and variability; Diliberto said it was vetted and “only after the end of the first quarter,” and future variability is driven by “distributions.”

  • Burrows, Goldman Sachs: asked SUMMIT expectations; Holliday said Q3 should show “some good numbers” and added, “I think we're going to have a very good second half of the year.”

  • Michael Lewis, Truist: asked AI tenant credit risk; Durels said AI exposure is “somewhere between 1% to 2% of the portfolio,” with key tenants “well capitalized.”

  • Lewis, Truist: asked about net effective rent growth transparency; Holliday said, “We don't track… net effective growth,” citing TI amortization complexity.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive with repeated probing on sustainability and cash flow visibility; the most pressing exchange was Malhotra on 2027 planning, ending with Diliberto: “I plan to do that in December… No.”

  • Management’s tone was positive and assertive in prepared remarks (Holliday: “I remain very optimistic”), while in Q&A it was confident on leasing/capital markets but occasionally firm or limiting on disclosure (Diliberto: “No.”).

  • Versus Q1, the tone shifted from “excellent quarter” and leasing records to a stronger emphasis on a “significant upward” guidance change and One Vanderbilt’s incremental FFO mechanics, with more pointed analyst questions on the quality/visibility of earnings.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The largest change from Q1 to Q2 was the explicit “significant upward FFO guidance revision of $1.20 a share,” including “an additional $0.80 a share” tied to One Vanderbilt accounting mechanics; Q1 focused on leasing volume records and business-plan progress without a comparable guidance step-up.

  • Strategic emphasis remained consistent on Midtown scarcity and conversions, but Q2 added more detail on investment sales execution status (“4 of the 11 deals”) and hedging posture (“more like 90-10” fixed-to-floating).

  • Analyst focus shifted from Q1 cadence/pipeline composition and dividend/FAD framing to Q2 scrutiny of the One Vanderbilt uplift’s variability, capital markets timing, and the company’s willingness to outline a 2027 plan.

Risks and Concerns

  • Rate and benchmark volatility remained a key issue; Sitomer said, “the benchmark… is not cooperating with us,” while management’s mitigation was hedging and maintaining a heavily fixed profile.

  • Development execution and neighbor-related litigation surfaced; Holliday described 346 Madison access litigation as “fairly… routine,” and DeWitt detailed “numerous layers of oversight” for 750 Third structural controls.

  • Tourism softness was acknowledged as a headwind; Holliday said, “foreign tourism particularly has been substandard,” while pointing to variable expense management and new ticketed experiences at SUMMIT.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized a step-change in 2026 earnings expectations, led by a $1.20-per-share upward FFO guidance revision that includes a $0.80-per-share recurring contribution tied to One Vanderbilt’s accounting treatment, while reiterating strengthening Manhattan leasing conditions, continued rent appreciation across key Midtown corridors, and ongoing capital recycling and financing execution under a hedged balance sheet posture.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Japan's manufacturing PMI beats forecasts at 54.7 in July; composite PMI hits highest level since February at 53.1
Australia's private sector activity accelerates in July as services & factory orders rebound
data.description:
Private sector activity in Australia accelerated in July 2026 to its highest level since January, propelled by a strong rebound in services activity and the first rise in new manufacturing orders in five months.

The S&P Global Australia Manufacturing PMI inched up to 51.7 from 51.5 in the prior month, marking a fourth consecutive month of expansion. Factory growth was underpinned by a return to new order growth, driven by stronger domestic demand and new client wins, while manufacturing employment expanded at its fastest pace since April.

The S&P Global Flash Australia Services PMI Business Activity Index increased to 53.0 in July 2026 from 50.5 in June, signaling a sharp pickup in services-sector expansion to a six-month high.

The S&P Global Flash Australia Composite PMI climbed to 52.6 in July from 50.4 in June, marking the strongest overall growth rate since January.

Despite the upbeat economic survey data, Australian financial markets pulled back around midday, the S&P/ASX 200 Index dipped 26 points, or 0.3%, to 8,813, reversing gains from the previous three sessions. The Australian dollar traded below $0.698 and remained on track for its first weekly decline in a month.
